description: Structured handoff interview conducted with a departing employee in their final two weeks. Captures formal and informal responsibilities, key relationships, system ownership, undocumented institutional knowledge, and risks — producing a role brief for the manager, the team, and the eventual backfill hire.

coverage_categories.name: role_summary
coverage_categories.description: The interviewee's own framing of what they actually did day-to-day, contrasted with their formal job description. Surfaces the gap between the role on paper and the role in practice.

coverage_categories.name: responsibilities
coverage_categories.description: Discrete recurring or ad-hoc responsibilities the interviewee owned, including informal ones never assigned in writing. Each item should capture criticality, frequency, and current handoff status.

coverage_categories.name: people
coverage_categories.description: Internal and external stakeholders the interviewee worked with regularly. Captures the relational and political landscape — who is critical to the work, who is difficult, and why.

coverage_categories.name: systems
coverage_categories.description: Software, platforms, scripts, spreadsheets, and ad-hoc tooling the interviewee owned, maintained, or relied on heavily. Captures fragility, documentation status, and access dependencies.

coverage_categories.name: implicit_knowledge
coverage_categories.description: Things the interviewee knows but has never written down — workarounds, conventions, historical context, judgment calls. Often invisible until the person is gone.

coverage_categories.name: risks
coverage_categories.description: Things the interviewee was quietly holding together, fragile relationships, technical debt they were aware of, and what would break first if no one took over their work.

coverage_categories.name: hiring_profile
coverage_categories.description: Skills, experience, and dispositions the interviewee thinks the backfill needs. Generated near the end of the interview as a synthesis, with input on red flags and interview questions.

role_summary: If you had to describe what you actually do day-to-day to someone who's never seen your job description, how would you put it?
Where does your formal job description match what you really spent your time on, and where does it not?
Walk me through a typical week — not what's on your calendar, but what actually fills your time.
If your role were posted tomorrow exactly as it's written, what would the candidate be unprepared for on day one?
responsibilities: Tell me about the last thing you did this week that nobody asked you to do, but that needed doing.
What's a recurring task you own that probably isn't written down anywhere?
Walk me through the last time something you're responsible for went sideways — what did you have to do to get it back on track?
Is there anything you handle that you suspect your manager doesn't fully realize you handle?
Of everything you do, what would be the first thing to fall through the cracks if you stopped showing up tomorrow?
people: Who do you talk to or message most days, and what do you usually need from them?
Tell me about the last time you had to work with someone difficult to get something done — who was it, and what did you have to navigate?
Outside this company, who are the people the next person in this role absolutely has to know about?
Is there anyone whose cooperation you've earned over time that a new person would have to earn from scratch?
Who would you warn the person taking over about — not in a mean way, just so they know what they're walking into?
systems: What systems or tools do you log into in a given day, and which of those would the next person need their own access to?
Tell me about the last time something broke or behaved weirdly in a system you use — what did you do?
Is there a spreadsheet, script, or workaround that you maintain that other people rely on without realizing you maintain it?
Which tool you use is the most fragile — the one where you hold your breath a little when you run it?
If your laptop disappeared tomorrow, what's not backed up or documented anywhere else?
implicit_knowledge: What's something you know about how things work here that you've never had to explain to anyone, because everyone you work with already knew it?
Tell me about the last time you gave advice to a newer colleague — what did you find yourself explaining?
Is there a 'we don't do it that way because of what happened in 2022' kind of story you carry around?
What's a judgment call you make routinely that you'd struggle to write down as a rule?
Where in the org chart do the lines on paper not match how decisions actually get made?
risks: What's the thing you've been quietly worrying about that you haven't gotten around to fixing?
If you had two more weeks and could only address one risk before you leave, what would it be?
Tell me about something that almost broke recently and didn't — what kept it from breaking?
Is there a relationship, system, or process where you're effectively the only safety net?
What would you tell the next person in your role to keep an eye on for the first ninety days?
hiring_profile: Knowing what you know now, what kind of background or experience would you look for in the person who replaces you?
What's a skill the job description probably emphasizes that actually doesn't matter that much?
What's a skill or trait the job description probably misses that's essential to surviving in this role?
If you were interviewing your own replacement, what's one question you'd ask to separate someone who will thrive from someone who'll struggle?
What red flag in a candidate would make you want to warn the hiring manager?

role_summary.item_description: A pair of statements: a one-line description of the role as actually practiced, plus an explicit delta between the formal job description and reality.

responsibilities.item_description: A discrete responsibility with a short title (imperative verb-noun phrase), a description of what it involves, criticality (low/medium/high/critical), frequency (daily/weekly/monthly/ad-hoc), whether it appears in the formal job description, and current handoff status.

people.item_description: A person with full canonical name (First Last), role/title, organization (internal team or external company), relationship type (collaborator/dependency/stakeholder/escalation), why they matter for continuity, and any nuance about working with them.

systems.item_description: A system or tool with full canonical name (Vendor Product format), ownership status (owned/co-owned/used), fragility rating, documentation status, who else has access, and any known gotchas.

implicit_knowledge.item_description: A discrete piece of undocumented knowledge categorized by type (historical_context/workaround/judgment_rule/social_norm/technical_detail), with an urgency flag indicating how soon the successor will need it.

risks.item_description: A specific risk with a short title, description, type (single_point_of_failure/relationship_risk/technical_debt/knowledge_gap), severity, likelihood, and recommended mitigation.

hiring_profile.item_description: A consolidated profile containing required skills, recommended interview questions, red flags, and a note on background fit. Generated as a synthesis at end of interview, not per-turn.

clarification_triggers.condition: Interviewee names a person by first name only or with an ambiguous reference (e.g. 'Sarah', 'the guy in finance', 'my counterpart at the vendor').
clarification_triggers.suggested_question_template: When you mentioned {entity}, can you give me their full name and where they sit in the org? I want to make sure the right person ends up in the handoff.

clarification_triggers.condition: Interviewee references a system, tool, or platform without specifying which instance or version (e.g. 'Salesforce', 'the database', 'our CRM').
clarification_triggers.suggested_question_template: When you say {entity}, do you mean a specific instance or environment? I want to make sure the next person knows exactly which system to log into.

clarification_triggers.condition: Interviewee describes a responsibility in passive or vague terms ('it gets handled', 'someone takes care of', 'we usually').
clarification_triggers.suggested_question_template: When you say that {entity} 'gets handled' — who specifically does it today, and is that you?

clarification_triggers.condition: Interviewee mentions a risk, fragile system, or single point of failure without saying what would actually break or who would feel it.
clarification_triggers.suggested_question_template: If {entity} did break, what would actually go wrong, and who would notice first?

clarification_triggers.condition: Interviewee discusses a person in evaluative or interpersonal terms without saying what to actually do about it.
clarification_triggers.suggested_question_template: What's the practical advice you'd give the next person about working with {entity}?

clarification_triggers.condition: Interviewee mentions a workaround or non-obvious behavior without saying why the official path doesn't work.
clarification_triggers.suggested_question_template: Why doesn't the standard approach work for {entity} — is there a history there the next person should know?

clarification_triggers.condition: Interviewee uses 'we' or 'the team' for a responsibility but hasn't clarified whether anyone besides them is currently doing it.
clarification_triggers.suggested_question_template: When you say 'we' handle {entity}, is anyone else actually doing this with you today, or has it become just you?

clarification_triggers.condition: Interviewee names a project, initiative, or recurring meeting without clarifying its current status (active/paused/wrapping up).
clarification_triggers.suggested_question_template: Where does {entity} stand right now — is it ongoing, winding down, or on hold?
